title: The Passion and Resurrection
objective: Understand the events of Jesus' death and resurrection as the fulfillment of the entire gospel of Matthew and the foundation of Christian faith

required_passages.text: When Jesus had finished all these sayings, he said to his disciples, 'You know that after two days the Passover is coming, and the Son of Man will be delivered up to be crucified.' Then the chief priests and the elders of the people gathered in the palace of the high priest, whose name was Caiaphas, and plotted together in order to arrest Jesus by stealth and kill him. Then one of the twelve, whose name was Judas Iscariot, went to the chief priests and said, 'What will you give me if I deliver him over to you?' And they paid him thirty pieces of silver. Now on the first day of Unleavened Bread the disciples came to Jesus, saying, 'Where will you have us prepare for you to eat the Passover?' Now as they were eating, Jesus took bread, and after blessing it broke it and gave it to the disciples, and said, 'Take, eat; this is my body.' And he took a cup, and when he had given thanks he gave it to them, saying, 'Drink of it, all of you, for this is my blood of the covenant, which is poured out for many for the forgiveness of sins.' Then Jesus went with them to a place called Gethsemane, and he said to his disciples, 'Sit here, while I go over there and pray.' And going a little farther he fell on his face and prayed, saying, 'My Father, if it be possible, let this cup pass from me; nevertheless, not as I will, but as you will.' While he was still speaking, Judas came, one of the twelve, and with him a great crowd with swords and clubs, from the chief priests and the elders of the people. And he came up to Jesus at once and said, 'Greetings, Rabbi!' And he kissed him. Then they seized Jesus and arrested him. But Peter was sitting outside in the courtyard. And a servant girl came up to him and said, 'You also were with Jesus the Galilean.' But he denied it before them all. And Peter remembered the saying of Jesus, 'Before the rooster crows, you will deny me three times.' And he went out and wept bitterly. When morning came, all the chief priests and the elders of the people took counsel against Jesus to put him to death. And they bound him and led him away and delivered him over to Pilate the governor. So when Pilate saw that he was gaining nothing, but rather that a riot was beginning, he took water and washed his hands before the crowd, saying, 'I am innocent of this man's blood; see to it yourselves.' Then he released for them Barabbas, and having scourged Jesus, delivered him to be crucified. And they crucified him and divided his garments among them by casting lots. And about the ninth hour Jesus cried out with a loud voice, saying, 'Eli, Eli, lema sabachthani?' that is, 'My God, my God, why have you forsaken me?' And Jesus cried out again with a loud voice and yielded up his spirit. And behold, the curtain of the temple was torn in two, from top to bottom. And the earth shook, and the rocks were split. When the centurion and those who were with him, keeping watch over Jesus, saw the earthquake and what took place, they were filled with awe and said, 'Truly this was the Son of God!' Now after the Sabbath, toward the dawn of the first day of the week, Mary Magdalene and the other Mary went to see the tomb. And behold, there was a great earthquake, for an angel of the Lord descended from heaven and came and rolled back the stone and sat on it. But the angel said to the women, 'Do not be afraid, for I know that you seek Jesus who was crucified. He is not here, for he has risen, as he said. Come, see the place where he lay. Then go quickly and tell his disciples that he has risen from the dead.' So they departed quickly from the tomb with fear and great joy, and ran to tell his disciples. And behold, Jesus met them and said, 'Greetings!' And they came up and took hold of his feet and worshiped him. Now the eleven disciples went to Galilee, to the mountain to which Jesus had directed them. And when they saw him they worshiped him, but some doubted. And Jesus came and said to them, 'All authority in heaven and on earth has been given to me. Go therefore and make disciples of all nations, baptizing them in the name of the Father and of the Son and of the Holy Spirit, teaching them to observe all that I have commanded you. And behold, I am with you always, to the end of the age.'

segments.audio_script: Welcome to lesson twelve, the final lesson in our study of the Gospel of Matthew. We have journeyed from the genealogy that connected Jesus to Abraham and David, through his birth, baptism, temptation, the Sermon on the Mount, his miracles, and his parables. Everything in Matthew has been building toward this. The Passover is two days away and Jesus tells his disciples plainly: the Son of Man is about to be delivered up to be crucified. Today we walk through the betrayal, the arrest, the crucifixion, the resurrection, and the command that sends the church into every nation on earth.

Key passages from Matthew chapters 26 through 28. When Jesus had finished all these sayings, he said to his disciples, You know that after two days the Passover is coming, and the Son of Man will be delivered up to be crucified. Then one of the twelve, whose name was Judas Iscariot, went to the chief priests and said, What will you give me if I deliver him over to you? And they paid him thirty pieces of silver. Now as they were eating, Jesus took bread, and after blessing it broke it and gave it to the disciples, and said, Take, eat; this is my body. And he took a cup, and when he had given thanks he gave it to them, saying, Drink of it, all of you, for this is my blood of the covenant, which is poured out for many for the forgiveness of sins. Then Jesus went with them to a place called Gethsemane, and going a little farther he fell on his face and prayed, saying, My Father, if it be possible, let this cup pass from me; nevertheless, not as I will, but as you will. While he was still speaking, Judas came, and with him a great crowd with swords and clubs. And he kissed him. Then they seized Jesus and arrested him. Peter was sitting outside in the courtyard. And a servant girl came up to him and said, You also were with Jesus the Galilean. But he denied it. And Peter remembered the saying of Jesus, Before the rooster crows, you will deny me three times. And he went out and wept bitterly. When morning came, they bound him and led him away and delivered him over to Pilate the governor. And having scourged Jesus, they delivered him to be crucified. And about the ninth hour Jesus cried out with a loud voice, saying, My God, my God, why have you forsaken me? And Jesus cried out again with a loud voice and yielded up his spirit. And behold, the curtain of the temple was torn in two, from top to bottom. When the centurion saw what took place, he said, Truly this was the Son of God! Now after the Sabbath, the angel said to the women, Do not be afraid, for I know that you seek Jesus who was crucified. He is not here, for he has risen, as he said. Come, see the place where he lay. So they departed quickly from the tomb with fear and great joy. And Jesus met them and said, Greetings! And Jesus came and said to his disciples, All authority in heaven and on earth has been given to me. Go therefore and make disciples of all nations, baptizing them in the name of the Father and of the Son and of the Holy Spirit, teaching them to observe all that I have commanded you. And behold, I am with you always, to the end of the age.

Jesus chose to die at Passover, the annual celebration of Israel's liberation from Egypt. This was not a coincidence of timing. The original Passover lamb was slaughtered and its blood applied to the doorposts so that judgment would pass over the household. Jesus institutes a new covenant meal at the Last Supper, identifying his body as bread broken and his blood as the blood of the covenant poured out for many for the forgiveness of sins. He is claiming to be the Passover lamb of the new and final exodus, liberating people not from Egypt but from sin and its consequences. The thirty pieces of silver paid to Judas fulfilled Zechariah 11:12-13. The cry of dereliction from the cross quoted Psalm 22:1. The torn temple curtain recalled the barrier between humanity and God's presence in the Holy of Holies. Matthew is narrating the death of Jesus within a dense web of prophetic fulfillment.

Four moments in these chapters demand close attention. The first is Gethsemane. Jesus asks the Father to let the cup pass if it be possible, but submits his will to the Father's. The cup is the Old Testament image for God's wrath poured out on sin. Jesus is not simply dreading physical death. He is about to absorb divine judgment in the place of sinners, and he knows it. His prayer is the most honest and costly act of submission in all of scripture. The second moment is the cry of dereliction — My God, my God, why have you forsaken me? This is not a cry of despair or lost faith. It is a quotation of Psalm 22, which begins in agony and ends in triumph. But more than that, it signals the real event on the cross: Jesus experiencing the God-forsakenness that is the penalty of sin, in the place of those who deserved it. The third moment is the torn curtain. The thick veil separating the Holy of Holies from the outer courts was torn from top to bottom at the moment of Jesus' death. The direction — from top to bottom — indicates divine action, not human. The barrier between God and humanity has been removed. The fourth moment is the Great Commission. Jesus rises and meets his disciples on a mountain, echoing the Sermon on the Mount and the transfiguration. He claims all authority in heaven and on earth. On the basis of that authority he sends them to all nations.

Three great themes converge in these final chapters. The first is substitutionary sacrifice. Jesus dies in the place of others. His blood is poured out for the forgiveness of sins. Barabbas, the guilty man, is released while the innocent man is executed. Barabbas is every person who has ever been in the place of deserving judgment. The second theme is vindication through resurrection. The resurrection is not an appendix to the crucifixion. It is the Father's declaration that the Son has fully satisfied the demands of justice and that death has no more claim on him. The empty tomb is not primarily a comfort, though it is that. It is first of all a verdict. The third theme is universal mission. Matthew began with a genealogy that included Gentile women and ended with a commission to disciple all nations. The gospel that began in Bethlehem is now addressed to the ends of the earth, with the promise that the one who sends is present in the going.

Review question: What three commands does Jesus give his disciples in the Great Commission, and on what basis does he give them? Answer: He commands them to go, to baptize, and to teach. He gives these commands on the basis that all authority in heaven and on earth has been given to him.
segments.correct_answer: Go, baptize, and teach — on the basis of all authority in heaven and on earth being given to him
acceptable_variants: make disciples baptize and teach on the basis of his all authority
go baptize and teach all nations because all authority is his
the three commands are go baptize teach; grounded in his full authority
he commands going baptizing and teaching because all authority has been given to him

segments.audio_script: You have completed lesson twelve and with it the Gospel of Matthew. From the genealogy that placed Jesus at the culmination of Israel's history, through his birth, his testing, the Sermon on the Mount, his miracles, his parables, and now his death and resurrection, Matthew has made one sustained argument: Jesus is the Messiah, the Son of God, Immanuel — God with us — and he is with his people always, to the end of the age. Congratulations on completing this course.

vocabulary.definition: An Old Testament image for the outpouring of God's wrath on sin; when Jesus prays that the cup might pass, he is facing the prospect of bearing divine judgment in the place of sinners
Jesus' final command in Matthew to make disciples of all nations through going, baptizing, and teaching — grounded in his post-resurrection claim to all authority in heaven and on earth
